result.markdown:
--- title: "Model 3 вЂ” Neural Net" subtitle: "A small multilayer perceptron with nnet вЂ” and why scale matters" --- > **Where we are:** Last of three model documents in the > [Titanic вЂ” Tidymodels Tour](../index.qmd). Same structure as the > [XGBoost](02-xgboost.qmd) and [Random Forest](03-random-forest.qmd) docs вЂ” > recipe в†’ spec в†’ workflow в†’ tune в†’ finalize в†’ evaluate в†’ save. The one twist: > a neural net needs **normalized** inputs, so this doc extends the shared > `make_recipe()` with a normalization step. ## Why a neural net here? On a dataset this small (891 rows) a multilayer perceptron will not beat the tree ensembles вЂ” and that's fine. The point is to *explore* a different model family and see how tidymodels makes swapping engines almost free. A small MLP with a single hidden layer and weight decay (`penalty`) is a clean, instructive choice. ::: {.callout-note} ## Engine choice: `nnet` We fit the MLP with base R's `nnet` engine. It has no native-code dependency, so it trains identically on your laptop, in the Quarto site build, and in GitHub Actions CI. A `torch`-backed alternative (`brulee`) is shown later as an optional local upgrade вЂ” but it does **not** load inside this project's render/CI session, so `nnet` is the engine we publish. ::: ## Setup ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} source(here::here("R", "00_setup.R")) # tidyverse + tidymodels, seed = 42 source(here::here("R", "02_features.R")) # make_recipe(data) library(nnet) # the MLP engine (base R, no native deps) library(vip) # variable importance plots ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} train <- readr::read_csv(here::here("data", "raw", "train.csv")) |> dplyr::mutate(Survived = factor(Survived, levels = c("0", "1"))) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Tear down the PSOCK cluster from 00_setup.R and tune single-process. The grid # is tiny, so this is plenty fast and keeps results deterministic (no worker # package-loading surprises). if (exists("cl")) try(parallel::stopCluster(cl), silent = TRUE) foreach::registerDoSEQ() ``` ::: ## The recipe вЂ” with normalization Tree ensembles are invariant to monotone rescaling, so the shared recipe didn't bother normalizing. A neural net is **not** invariant: gradient descent behaves far better when every numeric predictor has mean 0 and unit variance, otherwise large-scale features (like `Fare`) dominate the gradient. So we take the shared recipe and append `step_normalize()`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Extend the shared recipe with normalization of all numeric predictors. This # step must come AFTER dummy encoding in make_recipe() so the 0/1 dummy columns # are normalized too (standard practice for MLPs). nnet_recipe <- make_recipe(train) |> step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) nnet_recipe ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` в”Ђв”Ђ Recipe в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` в”Ђв”Ђ Inputs ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Number of variables by role ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` outcome: 1 predictor: 10 id: 1 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` в”Ђв”Ђ Operations ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Variable mutation for: str_extract(Name, "(?<=, )[A-Za-z]+(?=\\.)"), ... ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Collapsing factor levels for: Title ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Variable mutation for: SibSp + Parch + 1L, ... ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Variable mutation for: str_sub(Cabin, 1, 1), ... ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Variables removed: Name, Ticket, Cabin ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў K-nearest neighbor imputation for: Age ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Mode imputation for: Embarked ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Median imputation for: Fare ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Variable mutation for: as.character(Pclass) ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Novel factor level assignment for: all_nominal_predictors() ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Dummy variables from: all_nominal_predictors() ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Zero variance filter on: all_predictors() ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` вЂў Centering and scaling for: all_numeric_predictors() ``` ::: ::: ## The model specification ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Single-hidden-layer MLP via base R's nnet engine: one hidden layer # (hidden_units) and L2 weight decay (penalty). nnet has no native-code # dependency, so it trains and renders reliably everywhere вЂ” including the # Quarto site build and GitHub Actions CI. nnet_spec <- mlp( hidden_units = tune(), penalty = tune() ) |> set_engine("nnet") |> set_mode("classification") nnet_spec ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Single Layer Neural Network Model Specification (classification) Main Arguments: hidden_units = tune() penalty = tune() Computational engine: nnet ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Optional: a torch-backed MLP with `brulee` (local only) For a deeper net with a tuned epoch budget you can swap in `brulee` (a `torch` backend) **when running locally**. It also exposes `epochs`: ```r nnet_spec <- mlp( hidden_units = tune(), penalty = tune(), epochs = tune() # brulee only ) |> set_engine("brulee") |> set_mode("classification") ``` We keep `nnet` as the published engine on purpose: `brulee`'s libtorch backend does not load inside the Quarto/knitr render session (or in CI) under this project's `renv` setup вЂ” every fit fails with *"Lantern is not loaded"*. `nnet` sidesteps that entirely. If you switch to `brulee`, add `epochs` back to the tuning grid below and train sequentially (`foreach::registerDoSEQ()`). ::: ## The workflow ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} nnet_wf <- workflow() |> add_recipe(nnet_recipe) |> add_model(nnet_spec) ``` ::: ## Tuning with 5-fold cross-validation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} set.seed(42) folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 5, strata = Survived) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Modest grid for a tiny dataset: a handful of hidden units and light-to-moderate # weight decay. (nnet has no epochs hyperparameter вЂ” it iterates to convergence.) set.seed(42) nnet_grid <- grid_space_filling( hidden_units(range = c(2L, 16L)), penalty(range = c(-4, -0.5)), # log10 scale в†’ 1e-4 to ~0.3 size = 12 ) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} nnet_metrics <- metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy) set.seed(42) nnet_tuned <- tune_grid( nnet_wf, resamples = folds, grid = nnet_grid, metrics = nnet_metrics, # Positive class "1" is the second factor level. control = control_grid(save_pred = TRUE, event_level = "second") ) ``` ::: ### Tuning results ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} autoplot(nnet_tuned, metric = "roc_auc") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![ROC-AUC across the MLP tuning grid (autoplot).](04-neural-net_files/figure-html/tune-plot-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} show_best(nnet_tuned, metric = "roc_auc", n = 5) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ::: ## Finalize and fit on the full training set ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} best_nnet <- select_best(nnet_tuned, metric = "roc_auc") best_nnet ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} final_nnet_wf <- finalize_workflow(nnet_wf, best_nnet) set.seed(42) nnet_fit <- fit(final_nnet_wf, data = train) ``` ::: ## Cross-validated performance ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # nnet tunes hidden_units + penalty (no epochs). If you switch to the brulee # engine, add "epochs" to the join keys. collect_metrics(nnet_tuned) |> dplyr::semi_join(best_nnet, by = c("hidden_units", "penalty")) |> dplyr::select(.metric, mean, std_err, n) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-tip} Copy the `accuracy` and `roc_auc` `mean` values into the results table on the [master document](../index.qmd). ::: ### Confusion matrix ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} nnet_cv_preds <- collect_predictions(nnet_tuned, parameters = best_nnet) nnet_cv_preds |> conf_mat(truth = Survived, estimate = .pred_class) |> autoplot(type = "heatmap") + ggplot2::labs(title = "Neural Net вЂ” CV confusion matrix") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Confusion matrix from out-of-fold CV predictions.](04-neural-net_files/figure-html/confusion-1.png){width=480} ::: ::: ### Variable importance Neural nets don't expose importance natively. For an `nnet` model `vip` derives it from the trained connection weights (Garson's algorithm) вЂ” no shuffling or prediction wrapper needed, so it's robust in any render environment. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Pull the underlying nnet engine object out of the fitted workflow and let vip # read importance straight from the network weights. nnet_fit |> extract_fit_engine() |> vip::vip(num_features = 15) + ggplot2::labs(title = "Neural Net variable importance (Garson weights)") + ggplot2::theme_minimal() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Connection-weight (Garson) importance for the MLP.](04-neural-net_files/figure-html/vip-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: Even through a different lens, the same features carry the weight вЂ” `Title`, `Sex`, class, `Fare` вЂ” consistent with [XGBoost](02-xgboost.qmd) and the [Random Forest](03-random-forest.qmd). ## Save the fitted workflow ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Project convention: the neural-net model saves to nnet_fit.rds. saveRDS(nnet_fit, file = here::here("models", "nnet_fit.rds")) ``` ::: ## Submission Generate this model's leaderboard CSV with the [parameterized submission document](submission.qmd): ```bash quarto render docs/submission.qmd -P model:nnet ``` That loads `models/nnet_fit.rds`, predicts on `data/raw/test.csv`, writes `models/submission_nnet.csv`, and prints the exact `kaggle competitions submit` command. See the [Kaggle setup guide](setup-kaggle.md) for authentication. ## Next steps - You've now fit all three families вЂ” compare their CV scores side by side in the **[master results table](../index.qmd)**. - Revisit **[XGBoost](02-xgboost.qmd)** and the **[Random Forest](03-random-forest.qmd)**. - Build the CSV with the **[Submission doc](submission.qmd)** and push it to the leaderboard.
